title: Reshma Saujani on the Childcare Affordability Crisis
content:
The grand pageantry of King Charles III’s recent visit to the United States stood in stark contrast to the grim reality of life back in Britain. As the King enjoyed a White House state dinner and addressed Congress with typical poise, his home country grappled with a profound sense of decline. Over the past two years, the language of deterioration has spilled completely into the British mainstream. Politicians across the spectrum, from the center-left to the populist right, now openly describe the nation using terms of rot and failure. This widespread pessimism suggests that the country's domestic problems have simply become too obvious to hide.

The root of much of this anxiety can be traced back to the painful and unresolved Brexit debates of 2016. Since leaving the European Union, the promised economic benefits have failed to materialize, leaving many citizens feeling poorer and left behind. Labour Prime Minister Keir Starmer has warned of a societal black hole, while opposition leaders bluntly state that Britain is failing to compete globally. Furthermore, the populist Reform UK party has built a highly successful campaign around the simple, devastating slogan: 'Britain is broken.' Recent local elections proved that this message is translating directly into significant votes.

This collective diagnosing of Britain reflects a nation struggling to find its footing in a fractured world. While the royal motorcades and white-tie events abroad project an image of enduring imperial strength, the domestic reality is one of crumbling public services and economic stagnation. Leaders recognize that there is a political advantage to acknowledging this deterioration rather than pretending it does not exist. However, identifying the problem has proven much easier than finding a viable solution. As the political center struggles to hold, the nation remains deeply divided over how to reverse its long, slow decline.
